title: Return of the Jedi (radio)

'''''Return of the Jedi''''' is a six-part radio adaptation of {{Film|VI}}, the third of the [[Star Wars radio dramas|''Star Wars'' radio dramatizations]] adapting the [[original trilogy]]. The drama was produced by [[HighBridge Audio]], the company that had released the first two series on tape and CD, in association with [[Tom Voegeli]] Productions and NPR's L.A. Theatre Works. It was broadcast on [[National Public Radio]] in the US over six weeks in late [[1996]].

The drama came more than a decade after the first two radio series, [[Star Wars (radio)|''Star Wars'']] ([[1981]]) and [[The Empire Strikes Back (radio)|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] ([[1983]]). Federal funding cuts had made it impossible for NPR to produce the third installment of the trilogy, but strong sales for recordings of the first two dramas convinced HighBridge to produce a new radio adaptation.<ref name="Robb">{{BookCite|author=Brian J. Robb|title=A Brief Guide to Star Wars |publisher=Hachette |date=2012 |isbn=9781780335834}}</ref> Once again [[Lucasfilm]] donated the rights and allowed the use of the film's sound effects and [[John Williams]]' musical score. Most of the cast and crew from the first two dramas were able to return, with the notable exception of ''Star Wars'' film actors [[Mark Hamill]] and [[Billy Dee Williams]], whose roles had to be recast. The script was the final project for veteran ''Star Wars'' writer [[Brian Daley]], who died the day that recording wrapped up.<ref name="Dramatization" /> As with the other radio dramas, ''Return of the Jedi'' adds new scenes and dialogue to material adapted from the film, including multiple references to the growing [[Star Wars Legends|''Star Wars'' Expanded Universe]].

==Plot summary==
Like the first two installments in the trilogy, ''Return of the Jedi'' for radio follows the events of the film that it is based on. [[C-3PO/Legends|See-Threepio]], [[R2-D2/Legends|Artoo-Detoo]], [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa]], and [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]] carry out an elaborate plan, orchestrated by [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]], for the [[Rescue of Han Solo/Legends|rescue of Han Solo]] from the [[Jabba's Palace/Legends|palace]] of [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]]. After defeating Jabba, the friends unite with the [[Alliance Fleet/Legends|Rebel fleet]] before embarking on a mission to infiltrate the [[Endor/Legends|Forest Moon of Endor]] and attack the [[Palpatine/Legends|Emperor's]] new [[Death Star II/Legends|Death Star]]. The drama culminates with the [[Battle of Endor/Legends|Battle of Endor]] in the forest, in space, and in the [[Emperor's Throne Room/Legends|Emperor's Throne Room]]. 

The plot of the radio drama includes some original events not in the film, but due to its short, six-episode run, these are relatively brief scenes. The first episode opens with a depiction of Luke in [[Ben Kenobi's home/Legends|Ben Kenobi's home]] finishing the construction of his [[Luke Skywalker's green lightsaber/Legends|new lightsaber]], the last step of his training to become a [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]]. Another scene depicts the heroes' escape from the planet [[Tatooine/Legends|Tatooine]] in their starships, the final step of their rescue mission. Some new dialogue makes connections to newly-published stories from the [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]]. These include a cameo appearance by [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]] in her disguise as the dancer Arica;<ref name="Tales">''[[Tales from Jabba's Palace]]''</ref> descriptions of events from [[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire (novel)|''Shadows of the Empire'']], which was published a few months before the drama aired and takes place just before it;<ref name="SotE">[[Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire (novel)|''Shadows of the Empire'' novel]]</ref> and a foreshadowing of the return of [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]] in the ''[[Star Wars: Dark Empire|Dark Empire]]'' comic series.<ref name="Dark Empire">[[Dark Empire 4|''Dark Empire'' 4]]</ref>

==Development==
===Conception===
Creation of the radio version of ''Return of the Jedi'' was delayed 13 years due to Congressional cuts in spending for public broadcasting. While NPR did not have funding to produce the drama, high sales for the compact disc and cassette releases of the ''Star Wars'' and ''The Empire Strikes Back'' radio dramas convinced executives at Highbridge Audio that their studio could produce the drama of ''Return of the Jedi''.<ref name="Robb" />

The bulk of the production crew and core cast from the first two dramas were able to return: scriptwriter [[Brian Daley]], director [[John Madden]], and producer [[Tom Voegeli]], as well as actors [[Anthony Daniels]], [[Perry King]], [[Ann Sachs]], [[Brock Peters]], [[Paul Hecht]], [[Bernard Behrens]], and [[John Lithgow]]. Two of the series' three actors from the ''Star Wars'' films, however, did not reprise their roles. [[Joshua Fardon]] took over as [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] and [[Arye Gross]] took the role of [[Lando Calrissian/Legends|Lando Calrissian]]. [[Ed Begley, Jr.]] was cast as the voice of [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]], while [[Ed Asner]], speaking only in grunts, guest-starred as [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]]. 

Brian Daley was ill with pancreatic cancer when work on the drama began, so HighBridge contracted [[John Whitman]] to do the inevitable rewriting that would come after the director and cast began working with the script. Whitman had experience writing audio dramas for the ''Star Wars'' Expanded Universe, including ''[[Dark Empire (audio drama)|Dark Empire]]'' and ''[[Tales of the Jedi (audio drama)|Tales of the Jedi]]''. However, when Madden and the actors began their read-throughs of Daley's script, they found that very little rewriting was necessary and that his unaltered script would serve in most cases. Whitman is credited as a co-writer only for the first two episodes. One notable rewrite involved a conversation between [[C-3PO/Legends|Threepio]] and Boba Fett that provided exposition about Jabba's Palace. These scenes made Fett's character seem excessively friendly, so the bounty hunter was replaced with [[Timothy Zahn]]'s character [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]], in disguise as the dancer Arica.<ref name="Dramatization" />

===Production===
''Return of the Jedi'' was recorded at the same pace as its two predecessors: one episode per day, for a total of six rather grueling days. The cast and crew returned to Westlake Audio Studios in Los Angeles, where the first ''Star Wars'' drama had been recorded.<ref name="Dramatization" />

Tom Voegeli again realized the sound mixing at his home base in St Paul, Minnesota.<ref name="Dramatization" /> He and his team again had access to the sounds and music from the films. The dialogue for [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacaca]], [[R2-D2/Legends|Artoo]], [[Nien Nunb/Legends|Nien Nunb]], and the [[Ewok/Legends|Ewoks]] all came from [[Ben Burtt]]'s sound library and were added to the newly-recorded dialogue in post-production.<ref name="Dramatization" /> The musical score included the songs "[[Lapti Nek/Legends|Lapti Nek]]" and "[[Ewok Celebration]]," which would be replaced the following year in the [[The Star Wars Trilogy Special Edition|Special Edition]] by "[[Jedi Rocks/Legends|Jedi Rocks]]" and "[[Victory Celebration]]".<ref name="fitting">{{WebCite|url=https://www.stepbystep.com/Jedi-Adaptation-is-a-Fitting-Conclusion-to-Star-Wars-Radio-Drama-Trilogy-136524/ |text=Jedi Adaptation is a Fitting Conclusion to Star Wars Radio Drama Trilogy |work=StepByStep |archivedate=20240328200757}}</ref>

==Release and reception==
NPR broadcast the first episode, "[[Tatooine Haunts]]," on November 5, 1996, with subsequent episodes airing weekly on Tuesdays. The finale "[[Blood of a Jedi]]" aired on December 10. Some local stations followed different schedules.<ref name="Kushner" /> Before the end of the year, HighBridge released a Collector's Limited Edition of [[Star Wars radio dramas|the entire radio trilogy]]. 

The radio drama's original script was published in ''[[Return of the Jedi: The National Public Radio Dramatization]]'', also in December 1996. Anthony Daniels stepped in for Brian Daley to write the introduction. Along with Daley's script, the book contains production photos, relevant concept art from the film, and a transcript of the get well message that the cast made for Daley on the last day of recording.<ref name="Dramatization" />
